system_prompt: You are an analyst. Read the brief carefully and provide your best-reasoned recommendation.
user_prompt:
# Task C - Health app data privacy policy

## Brief

A direct-to-consumer health app (~2M monthly active users) is launching an AI-powered health coaching feature. The product team must choose a primary data policy that governs how user health data is processed by the AI. The choice has implications for capability, privacy, regulatory exposure, cost, and trust. The CEO asks for a primary recommendation.

## Options (elements E1-E5, anonymized later)

**Option A:** Local-only processing. All AI inference happens on the user's device. No health data leaves the phone. The model is smaller (capability ceiling 60-70% of cloud-equivalent) but privacy is structurally maximal.

**Option B:** Encrypted cloud with no training. User health data sent to the cloud encrypted, decrypted only at inference time in a confidential-computing enclave, never used to train any model. Full capability, ~3x infrastructure cost.

**Option C:** Cloud with opt-out training. Data sent to the cloud, used for personalization by default, used for model improvement unless user opts out. Full capability, standard cost, regulatory grey area in some jurisdictions.

**Option D:** Cloud with opt-in training only. Data sent to the cloud and used for personalization; used for model improvement only with explicit opt-in. Default-on opt-in flow with clear UX. Capability slightly degraded vs C by default (less training data).

**Option E:** Federated learning. Personalization happens locally; aggregate model updates sent to cloud, individual data does not leave the device. Privacy preserved at architectural level. Capability and reliability still under active research; deployment complexity is high.

## Task for each agent

Recommend the most appropriate policy and explain why. 200-400 words. Advocate a position rather than enumerating all options.

## Why this task

- Different domain from A (capital allocation) and B (organizational diagnosis): product ethics with regulatory and technical dimensions.
- Multi-axis tradeoffs: privacy / capability / cost / trust / complexity.
- No clear right answer; reasonable agents will weight axes differently.
- 5 elements suitable for triadic procedure.
- Cross-validates whether method works on a third domain not seen in pilot.


Provide your recommendation in 200-400 words. Advocate for a specific position; do not enumerate all options.
raw_response_content:
# Recommendation: Option B — Encrypted cloud with confidential-computing enclaves, no training

For a direct-to-consumer health app at 2M MAU launching an AI coaching feature, **Option B is the right primary policy**, and the 3x infrastructure cost is the price of doing this category responsibly.

The decisive consideration is the asymmetry of downside risk. Health data is among the most sensitive categories under GDPR (Article 9), HIPAA-adjacent norms, and emerging state laws (e.g., Washington's My Health My Data Act). A single training-data leakage incident — model memorization, subpoena exposure, or a breach revealing that intimate health queries were used to improve a product — is an extinction-level trust event for a consumer health brand. Options C and D both keep that risk on the table; C is actively reckless given the regulatory grey area, and D's "default-on opt-in" is the kind of dark-pattern-adjacent design that regulators and journalists are increasingly hostile to.

Option A (local-only) is structurally appealing but ships a materially weaker product. A 60-70% capability ceiling in a coaching context means worse triage, worse personalization, and a real safety floor problem: under-capable health advice has its own harm profile. Privacy maximalism that degrades clinical usefulness is not a free win.

Option E (federated learning) is the right *eventual* architecture but not the right *launch* policy. "Capability and reliability still under active research" plus "high deployment complexity" is disqualifying for a v1 governing 2M users' health data. Ship B, prototype E in parallel, migrate when mature.

Option B threads the needle: full model capability, structural commitment that user data is never training fodder, and a confidential-computing story that is **legible to regulators, auditable by third parties, and marketable to users.** "Your data is decrypted only inside a sealed enclave and is never used to train our models" is a sentence the legal, marketing, and engineering teams can all stand behind without asterisks.

The 3x infrastructure cost sounds large in isolation but is small relative to (a) CAC in consumer health, (b) the expected cost of one serious privacy incident, and (c) the premium pricing and retention that a credible privacy posture supports. Treat it as a moat, not overhead.

**Recommend B as primary policy; roadmap E as successor architecture.**
